problem_statement: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across hospitals without centralizing patient data. However, existing clinical FL frameworks implicitly assume that all participating sites possess identical data modalities (e.g., both T1 and T2 MRI scans). In real-world multi-center collaborations, equipment availability and clinical protocols vary drastically, leading to severe modality imbalances. Current aggregation methods fail to harness cross-modal representations effectively when certain modalities are entirely missing from a client.
proposed_methodology: We propose FedMod, a novel federated learning framework designed explicitly for severe modality imbalance. We will utilize the BraTS 2021 and MIMIC-CXR datasets, partitioning them to simulate clients with missing modalities. We will evaluate our approach using Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) against standard FedAvg and FedProx baselines using PyTorch and Flower.
evaluation_plan: The framework will be evaluated across three axes: 1) Segmentation performance (DSC) under varying rates of missing modalities, 2) Communication efficiency (rounds to convergence), and 3) Resilience to heterogenous hospital data distributions.
expected_contribution: This project will deliver the first open-source federated learning aggregation strategy that mathematically guarantees performance bounds even when 50% of participating clients are missing one or more primary data modalities.
timeline: Months 1-2: Data curation and centralized baseline training. Months 3-4: Implementation of the proposed FedMod aggregation strategy in Flower. Month 5: Extensive ablation studies on modality dropping. Month 6: Paper writing and open-source release.
budget_estimate: $15,000 for cloud computing resources (Multi-GPU instances for simulating 10+ clients) and $50,000 for graduate student researcher stipend.
